unitTitle: KS1 Y2 Programming quizzes (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: ScratchJr recap
lessons.objectives:
I can find the parts of the ScratchJr screen
I can find the start of a sequence
I can see that a program needs to be started
I can run my program
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on the board: sequence · command · program · run · start. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; ScratchJr open on each tablet. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — recap the ScratchJr screen. On the starter worksheet pupils LABEL the screen (the stage, the green flag, the blocks).
   • Support: choose what starts a program.  • Core: choose what the stage is.  • Challenge: write one thing you can do on the screen.
2. (10–20) I-do — a sequence is things that happen in order, and every sequence has a start (the school day: gate opens → line up → walk to class). Likely error: naming a later step as the start → fix-words "the start is the very first thing."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the school-day sequence, then learn that a program does not move until you start it (the green flag).
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils build a tiny program and run it full screen with the green flag, then screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil shows their green flag.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine; usable by a non-specialist TA.

lessons.title: Outcomes
lessons.objectives:
I can predict the outcome of a sequence of commands
I can match two sequences with the same outcome
I can change the outcome of a sequence of commands
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: sequence · command · outcome · predict · program · blocks. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; ScratchJr open with the cat. Read the three "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is an outcome? (clap → a noise; pour juice and water → a drink). On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE the outcome of an everyday sequence.
   • Support: choose the outcome of a clap.  • Core: choose the outcome of the juice steps.  • Challenge: say the outcome of baking a cake.
2. (10–20) I-do — a program is a sequence of commands and running it has an outcome; read the blocks and PREDICT before you run. Likely error: guessing without reading → fix-words "read the blocks in order, left to right."
3. (20–35) We-do — two programs use the same blocks in a different order and end the same way (the same outcome). On the activity worksheet pupils SORT same-outcome from different-outcome, then MATCH the programs that do the same thing.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils start a new project and add blocks to send the cat somewhere new (change the outcome), then screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three "I can…"; each pupil names one outcome they made.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Using a design
lessons.objectives:
I can use the Start on tap block
I can work out the actions of a sprite in an algorithm
I can decide which blocks to use to meet the design
I can build the sequence of blocks I need
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: sprite · algorithm · blocks · design · sequence · predict. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; ScratchJr open with the cat. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — a new way to start: the Start on tap block runs when you tap the sprite. On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE how Start on tap works.
   • Support: choose how to start (tap the sprite).  • Core: choose that both blocks start a program.  • Challenge: say why tapping the sprite is useful.
2. (10–20) I-do — show a design (background · sprite · algorithm) and read the seasons design together. Model finding the Say and Change page blocks. Likely error: pressing the flag instead of tapping the sprite → fix-words "this one needs a tap on the sprite."
3. (20–35) We-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the algorithm (tap the cat → say the season → change the background) and SORT each block to its job.
4. (35–50) You-do — pupils build the seasons animation from the design (spring → summer → autumn → winter) and screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil taps their cat.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Changing a design
lessons.objectives:
I can choose backgrounds for the design
I can choose characters for the design
I can create a program based on the new design
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: sprite · project · blocks · design · sequence · change. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; ScratchJr open. Read the three "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — look at the "Who lives here?" quiz (sea background, a cat and a fish). On the starter worksheet pupils LABEL the quiz screen (cat, fish, the program) and choose which animal lives in the sea.
   • Support: choose which animal lives in the sea.  • Core: choose the quiz question.  • Challenge: name another sprite that could live there.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — the right answer says "Yes" then changes to the next question; the wrong answer says "No" and stops. Compare the two codes. Likely error: thinking the wrong answer also changes the background → fix-words "the wrong answer just says No and stops."
3. (25–40) You-do part 1 — on the activity worksheet pupils RECORD their background and two sprites (sprites that live in the background), and a multi-select on what the wrong-answer code does.
4. (40–50) You-do part 2 — pupils build their quiz question in ScratchJr and screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three "I can…"; each pupil says their quiz question.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Designing and creating
lessons.objectives:
I can choose the images for my own design
I can create an algorithm
I can build sequences of blocks to match my design
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: design · algorithm · build · sequence · blocks · match. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; ScratchJr open; design sheets ready. Read the three "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — choose your own pictures: the background and the sprites should go together (a space background with space sprites). On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE that sprites should match the background.
   • Support: choose that sprites match the background.  • Core: write the background they will use.  • Challenge: write a question that fits their background.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — an algorithm is the ordered steps (tap → say "Yes" → an action → change the background). Write one algorithm on the board. Likely error: steps out of order → fix-words "read it like a list, top to bottom."
3. (25–40) You-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the algorithm and SORT the actions (moving vs other), then build their quiz from their own design.
4. (40–50) Save — pupils SAVE their project (name it) so it can be reused in Lesson 6, and screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three "I can…"; each pupil shows their saved project.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Evaluating
lessons.objectives:
I can compare my project to my design
I can improve my project by adding features
I can debug my program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: compare · design · debug · program · features · evaluate. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; saved projects and design sheets handed back. Read the three "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — to evaluate is to check your work with two questions: does the design match my project, and can I improve it? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE what evaluate means.
   • Support: choose what evaluate means.  • Core: choose that a mismatch is OK to change.  • Challenge: say why comparing is useful.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — compare the design and the code side by side: the dog code matches (says "Yes!"); the cat code has a mistake (says "hi" not "Yes!") — circle and fix it. Key idea: debugging is finding and fixing a mistake. Likely error: not reading closely → fix-words "check every word against the design."
3. (25–40) You-do — on the activity worksheet pupils find the cat's mistake and say the fix, then improve their own quiz with a feature (the green Sound block).
4. (40–50) You-do — pupils check, debug and improve their own quiz, then screenshot the finished quiz.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three "I can…"; each pupil names one thing they fixed or added. Celebrate the finished quizzes (last lesson of the unit).
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.
